questionText: Find the angle between the hour hand and the minute hand of a clock when the time is 3:25.

steps.explanation: Calculate angle traced by the hour hand at 3 hrs 25 min = 3 + 25/60 = 41/12 hrs.
Calculate angle traced by the minute hand in 25 minutes.
Find the difference between the two angles.

solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to convert 3 hrs 25 min to a fractional hour for the hour hand calculation.

questionText: At what time between 2 and 3 o'clock will the hands of a clock be together?

steps.explanation: At 2 o'clock, the hour hand is at 2 and the minute hand is at 12, so they are 10 minute spaces apart.
The minute hand must gain 10 minute spaces over the hour hand.
In 60 minutes, the minute hand gains 55 minute spaces over the hour hand.
The hands coincide at this time after 2 o'clock.

solution.shortcut: Hands together between H and (H+1): time = $\frac{60H}{11}$ minutes past H. For H=2: $\frac{120}{11} = 10\frac{10}{11}$ min past 2.
solution.commonMistake: Thinking the minute hand gains 60 spaces in 60 minutes over the hour hand (it gains only 55).

questionText: At what time between 4 and 5 o'clock will the hands of a clock be at right angle?

steps.explanation: At 4 o'clock, the minute hand is 20 minute spaces behind the hour hand. For a right angle, the hands must be 15 minute spaces apart.
steps.calculation: Initial gap = 20 min spaces; right angle gap = 15 min spaces

steps.explanation: Case I: Minute hand is 15 min spaces BEHIND the hour hand. Minute hand gains (20 - 15) = 5 spaces.
Case II: Minute hand is 15 min spaces AHEAD of the hour hand. Minute hand gains (20 + 15) = 35 spaces.

solution.shortcut: Right angle times between H and (H+1): $t = \frac{60(H \pm 15)}{11}$ where + for Case II and − for Case I adjusted from initial gap.
solution.commonMistake: Missing the second case where the minute hand has overtaken the hour hand by 15 spaces.

questionText: Find at what time between 8 and 9 o'clock will the hands of a clock be in the same straight line but not together.

steps.explanation: At 8 o'clock, the hour hand is at 8 and the minute hand is at 12, so they are 20 minute spaces apart.
For hands in a straight line but not together (i.e., opposite), they must be 30 minute spaces apart.
Minute hand must gain (30 - 20) = 10 more spaces over the hour hand.

solution.shortcut: Opposite hands (straight line, not together) means 30 minute space gap. Gain needed = 30 − initial gap (if initial gap < 30).
solution.commonMistake: Confusing 'same straight line not together' (30 spaces apart) with 'together' (0 spaces apart).

questionText: At what time between 5 and 6 o'clock are the hands of a clock 3 minutes apart?

steps.explanation: At 5 o'clock, the minute hand is 25 minute spaces behind the hour hand.
Case I: Minute hand is 3 spaces BEHIND the hour hand. Minute hand must gain (25 - 3) = 22 spaces.
Case II: Minute hand is 3 spaces AHEAD of the hour hand. Minute hand must gain (25 + 3) = 28 spaces.

solution.shortcut: For d spaces apart between hour H: gain = (H×5 ± d), time = $\frac{60 \times \text{gain}}{55}$.
solution.commonMistake: Solving only one case and missing the second time when the hands are 3 minutes apart.

modelName: Clock hands at given distance apart

formulas.title: Time for hands d spaces apart

questionText: The minute hand of a clock overtakes the hour hand at intervals of 65 minutes of the correct time. How much does the clock gain or lose in a day?
correctAnswer: The clock gains $10\frac{10}{43}$ minutes in 24 hours.

steps.explanation: In a correct clock, the minute hand gains 55 min spaces over the hour hand in 60 min. To gain 60 min spaces (complete coincidence), it takes $\frac{60}{55} \times 60 = 65\frac{5}{11}$ min.
The given clock overtakes in only 65 min. Since $65 < 65\frac{5}{11}$, the clock gains time. Gain per coincidence = $65\frac{5}{11} - 65 = \frac{5}{11}$ min.
Calculate gain in 24 hours (= 1440 min).

solution.shortcut: Correct interval between coincidences = $65\frac{5}{11}$ min. Difference from given interval × (1440/given interval) gives daily gain/loss.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing gain vs loss: if given interval < $65\frac{5}{11}$, the clock gains; if greater, it loses.

questionText: A watch which gains uniformly is 5 min. slow at 8 o'clock in the morning on Sunday and it is 5 min. 48 sec. fast at 8 p.m. on the following Sunday. When was it correct?

steps.explanation: Time from 8 a.m. Sunday to 8 p.m. following Sunday = 7 days 12 hours = 180 hours.
Total gain = 5 min (slow→correct) + 5 min 48 sec (correct→fast) = 5 + $\frac{48}{60}$ min = $\frac{54}{5}$ min in 180 hours.

Time to gain 5 min (to become correct from −5 min slow): $\frac{180 \times 5}{\frac{54}{5}} = \frac{180 \times 5 \times 5}{54} = \frac{4500}{54} = 83\frac{1}{3}$ hours = 83 hrs 20 min = 3 days 11 hrs 20 min.
steps.result: 3 days 11 hrs 20 min after 8 a.m. Sunday

steps.explanation: 3 days 11 hrs 20 min after 8 a.m. Sunday = 8 a.m. Sunday + 3 days = 8 a.m. Wednesday + 11 hrs 20 min = 7:20 p.m. Wednesday.

solution.shortcut: Time to become correct = (initial slow/total gain) × total time.
solution.commonMistake: Not converting 5 min 48 sec to minutes correctly (48 sec = 4/5 min, so total = 5 + 5.8 = 10.8 min).

modelName: Watch gaining uniformly — find correct time

questionText: A clock is set right at 5 a.m. The clock loses 16 minutes in 24 hours. What will be the true time when the clock indicates 10 p.m. on 4th day?

steps.explanation: Time from 5 a.m. to 10 p.m. on 4th day = 3 × 24 + 17 = 89 hours by the clock.
The clock loses 16 min in 24 hrs, so 23 hrs 44 min of clock = 24 hrs of correct clock. Express 23 hrs 44 min as fraction: $23\frac{44}{60} = 23\frac{11}{15} = \frac{356}{15}$ hrs.
Convert 89 clock hours to correct hours.
90 hours after 5 a.m. = 3 days 18 hours after 5 a.m. = 11 p.m. on the 4th day.
steps.calculation: 5 \text{ a.m.} + 90 \text{ hrs} = 5 \text{ a.m.} + 3d18h = 11 \text{ p.m. on 4th day}

solution.shortcut: Correct time = Clock time × $\frac{24}{(24 - \text{loss per day in hrs})}$. Here 89 × $\frac{24}{(356/15)}$ = 90 hrs.
solution.commonMistake: Counting '4th day' incorrectly — from 5 a.m., day 1 ends at 5 a.m. next day; 10 p.m. on 4th day is 89 hrs later.

modelName: Clock losing time — find true time

questionText: A clock is set right at 8 a.m. The clock gains 10 minutes in 24 hours. What will be the true time when the clock indicates 1 p.m. on the following day?

steps.explanation: Time from 8 a.m. to 1 p.m. next day = 29 hours by the clock.
The clock gains 10 min in 24 hrs, so 24 hrs 10 min of this clock = 24 hrs correct. Express as fraction: $24\frac{10}{60} = 24\frac{1}{6} = \frac{145}{6}$ hrs.
Convert 29 clock hours to correct hours.
28 hrs 48 min after 8 a.m. = 8 a.m. + 28 hrs 48 min = 12:48 p.m. next day.

solution.commonMistake: Adding gain to clock time instead of converting; for a fast clock the true time is LESS than shown.

modelName: Clock gaining time — find true time

questionText: Rohan leaves home between 7 a.m. and 8 a.m. and returns between 1 p.m. and 2 p.m. to find that the minute and hour hands have interchanged their positions. How long was Rohan out of the house?

steps.explanation: When the hands interchange positions, they together make 7 complete revolutions (having crossed each other 6 times).
The hands interchange positions every $55\frac{5}{13}$ minutes (the time for the hands to swap after one revolution).
Required time = $7 \times 55\frac{5}{13}$ min.
Convert to hours and minutes: $387\frac{9}{13}$ min = 6 hrs $27\frac{9}{13}$ min.

solution.shortcut: Hands interchange → 7 complete rev. together. Each complete rev. by both takes $\frac{720}{13}$ min, so total = $7 \times \frac{720}{13}$.
solution.commonMistake: Assuming the hands interchange after just 1 revolution instead of counting correctly how many crossings occur between the given time windows.

formulas.title: Time for hands to complete 1 relative revolution
